preFix.title: Spinning Dorset Horn wool
preFix.subtitle: A bright white shortwool with good elasticity for sock yarn
preFix.excerpt: Dorset Horn has a fine-to-medium white fleece with a micron count of approximately 26-30 and a staple of 7-10 cm. Its good crimp gives the spun yarn springy memory, making it one of the better British breeds for sock-weight handspun. This guide covers washing, carding, spinning a fine singles, 2-ply construction, and wet-finishing under tension.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from British breed spinning references and Dorset Horn and Poll Dorset Sheep Breeders Association breed information. Published micron and staple figures used. The S-twist 2-ply and wet-finish under tension method is standard practice for sock-weight handspun. No single public domain text.

content.text: Dorset Horn is one of the oldest established British breeds, kept primarily for its dual-season breeding cycle and its white fleece. The raw fleece has a

of 7-10 cm. The crimp is good and consistent, and the spun yarn has a springy memory that holds its shape under wear. Of the shortwool Down-type breeds commonly available in the UK, Dorset Horn is among the few that spin reliably into a yarn with the elasticity sock construction requires.
Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. Tie back hair and roll up sleeves before sitting at the wheel.

Dorset Horn carries a moderate lanolin load. Wash in small batches in hot water (around 60°C) with washing-up liquid or a dedicated wool scouring product. Lower the locks into the water gently without agitating, leave for 15 minutes, lift out, and transfer to a plain hot rinse. Avoid letting the water cool around the wool in the wash basin; temperature shock does not felt the fleece directly, but moving it in and out of very different temperatures can shock the fibres. After the final rinse, press dry in a towel and spread on a mesh rack to dry. One scour is usually sufficient for Dorset Horn.

using hand cards. Load a small amount onto one card and brush back and forth three or four times until the fibres are evenly distributed and the batt looks fluffy with no clumps. Roll the batt off the card from the top down into a loose rolag. For sock-weight spinning, make the rolags slightly smaller and more compact than you would for a chunky yarn; a slimmer preparation means less material needs to be drafted at each pull.
To spin at sock weight, use a higher drive ratio (10:1 or more on most wheels) and treadle at a brisk, consistent pace. Keep the amount of fibre you release to the orifice small and steady. The

for a sock-weight 2-ply should be roughly 0.5-0.7 mm in diameter before plying, which feels extremely thin while you are spinning it. Check by wrapping the singles around a ruler: sock weight needs approximately 30-36 wraps per 2.5 cm.

Spin both singles bobbins with Z-twist (clockwise viewed from the orifice end of the wheel), keeping the twist angle and diameter as consistent as you can across both bobbins. Any variation in twist between the two bobbins will create an unbalanced 2-ply.
Place both bobbins on the lazy kate and

(anticlockwise wheel rotation). Keep the two singles under equal, light tension as you ply; if one feeds faster than the other, the ply will be uneven. Let the ply angle build to match the original spinning angle of the singles before allowing the wheel to draw in. A balanced 2-ply hangs straight and does not rotate when held at the midpoint.

Wind the plied yarn onto the niddy-noddy, tie the skein in four places, and soak in warm water for 20 minutes. When removing from the water, press dry in a towel rather than wringing. While the skein is still damp, hang it from one end and attach a gentle weight to the other: approximately 100-150 g for a 100 g skein. The weight stretches the skein under tension as it dries, locking in the ply twist and encouraging the crimp back into the fibres. This produces more spring and recovery in the finished yarn than drying flat. Remove the weight when the skein is fully dry.

items.problem: The singles are too thick for sock weight despite drafting thinly.
items.solution: Try a higher drive ratio setting, which puts more twist into a shorter length of fibre, allowing you to draft thinner before the twist locks the fibres. Also check that your rolags are not loaded too heavily; reducing the amount of fibre on the cards per pass will produce a slimmer preparation.

items.problem: The 2-ply is twisting and coiling rather than hanging straight.
items.solution: The ply twist is either too high or too low relative to the singles twist. Ply a short test length and let it fold back on itself: if it coils one way, reduce the ply twist; if it coils the other way, add more. Wet-finishing under tension will help relax minor imbalances.

items.problem: The finished sock yarn feels scratchy rather than soft.
items.solution: Some variability in softness occurs between individual fleeces even within a breed. Washing the finished knitted item with a lanolin-restoring wool wash often improves hand feel. If the micron count of the fleece was toward the upper end of the 26-30 range, the fibre will always feel slightly coarser than a lower-micron sample.

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
